question_text: Two identical balls are dropped from the same height. Ball A lands on a hard concrete surface and bounces to 60% of its original height. Ball B lands on soft foam and doesn't bounce at all, staying on the surface. For each ball, identify which energy stores are involved in the collision and explain why Ball B transfers more energy to the thermal store of its surroundings.
correct_answer: Ball A: gravitational potential → kinetic (before impact), then kinetic → gravitational potential + thermal (after bounce, with some energy lost). Ball B: gravitational potential → kinetic (before impact), then kinetic → thermal (after impact, no bounce). Ball B transfers more energy to thermal because all its kinetic energy is converted to heat/deformation in the foam, whereas Ball A retains some as gravitational potential energy when it bounces.
explanation: When objects collide, kinetic energy can be converted to thermal energy, sound energy, or other stores. Ball A bounces back, so some kinetic energy is converted back to gravitational potential energy. Ball B doesn't bounce, so nearly all its kinetic energy becomes thermal energy in the foam and ball, making it feel warm.
